mappings.rationaleAI: Core text on functional design covering the Arts and Crafts Movement, industrial design, and product form -- the central subject of this section (A, C.2).
Directly covers dress design and fashion accessories, explicitly listed under arts of functional design (C.1.a).
Covers typography, fonts, letterforms, and printing design, explicitly listed under design of materials for communication (C.4.b).
Covers advertising design including print media and outdoor advertising, directly relevant to advertising art and design (C.4.c).
Covers the design of brand identity including signs, symbols, and commercial marks, relevant to design for identification (C.4.d) and advertising design (C.4.c).
Covers handwriting systems, calligraphy, and lettering across cultures, directly relevant to C.4.a.
Covers Egyptian hieroglyphic writing and decorative inscription, relevant to calligraphy and lettering traditions (C.4.a).
Covers properties of clay, ceramics, glass, metals, and cement -- materials explicitly listed as decorative art media throughout section B.
Relevant to the integration of interior design with architecture, including ceilings, floors, walls, windows, and doors (C.3.b).
Modernist design philosophy transformed industrial and interior design, relevant to the relationship between art and functional objects (C.2, C.3).
Addresses aesthetic theory including beauty and taste, relevant to the nature and scope of decorative arts as a category distinct from fine art (A).
Classical Greek and Roman decorative arts including pottery, coins, medals, and temple ornamentation are relevant to B.1, B.5, and C.5.a.
Covers decorative traditions across cultures including body decoration, textiles, and ceremonial objects (C.1.c, B.10).
Prehistoric decorative arts including pottery, jewelry, and body decoration are among the earliest functional design objects (B.1, C.1).
Covers the properties of gemstones and decorative stones such as quartz and pyrite, relevant to gems as decorative materials (B.3).
Covers the chemistry of materials including ceramics, glass, and metals relevant to understanding decorative art media (B.1, B.4, B.5).
